# Local Ordered Touhou Arrangements Database

## Background

The Touhou Project is a long-running video game series developed solely by one individual, ZUN. Thanks to ZUN’s permissive stance on fan use of his game assets, the series has inspired a vibrant doujin (fan-made) scene. Among the most popular creations are music remixes, or "arrangements," of the original game soundtracks. These are produced by artists and groups known as “circles.”

I've been a fan of these arrangements since 2021 and have since amassed a large personal collection—both physical CDs and YouTube playlists. I organize these playlists based on how much I enjoy each track. Examples of such playlists include:

* [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NSAss0O44eg\&list=PLuDYUKEqeoaxodcKdDwsnjUBt1ZMsOa8q](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NSAss0O44eg&list=PLuDYUKEqeoaxodcKdDwsnjUBt1ZMsOa8q)  
* [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LP-cnbEwQeQ\&list=PLuDYUKEqeoay6kSIsCqY7KA58w--VNX7R](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LP-cnbEwQeQ&list=PLuDYUKEqeoay6kSIsCqY7KA58w--VNX7R)  
* [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ArMTUgUaV7k\&list=PLuDYUKEqeoazw-dcPeADU3rP0mfZ6nd3V](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ArMTUgUaV7k&list=PLuDYUKEqeoazw-dcPeADU3rP0mfZ6nd3V)

For some time, I've wanted to analyze my listening habits and preferences across these playlists. However, metadata around Touhou arrangements is notoriously inconsistent. YouTube uploaders often aren't the original creators and may include relevant information like the track name and circle in the title (e.g., “【東方ボーカル】 cry for the moon 【Poplica\*】”), in the description, or not at all (e.g., “東方 Touhou Trance 83”). The decentralized nature of the Touhou music scene makes identifying and cataloging tracks a significant challenge.

I’ve also dealt with issues like video takedowns breaking my playlists—a problem I’ve previously tackled with [this tool](https://github.com/AM22/archiver).

Recently, comprehensive Touhou music databases like [TouhouDB](https://touhoudb.com/), [Gensokyo Radio](https://gensokyoradio.net/search/), and [Touhou Arrangement Chronicle](https://touhou.arrangement-chronicle.com/detail_search) have made it easier to look up metadata for specific tracks. However, automatically linking YouTube videos from my playlists to their corresponding entries in these databases at scale remains an unsolved problem.

This inspired my new personal project: **L.O.T.A.D.** (Local Ordered Touhou Arrangements Database). The goal is to build a local database that stores and regularly syncs metadata for tracks in my YouTube playlists. By “local,” I mean existing on my local machine or an online resource private to me. By “ordered,” I mean able to assign songs and albums highly customizable scores and rankings. Once complete, this will allow me to run queries and gain insights into my listening patterns.

Additionally, since I have listened to so many Touhou songs, people often ask me for recommendations of a specific type (e.g. from a particular circle, arranged from a particular theme, or a specific genre). Currently, I resort to control f ing through a list of my playlist’s song names, which is unreliable. Once the database is constructed, these requests should be a single query away\!

## Requirements

I will split the requirements into high priority, medium priority, and low priority requirements. The initial design must address all high priority requirements. For medium and low priority requirements, we may not build them in the initial version of the system. For low priority requirements, we may only build them if feasible. Low priority includes some use cases I am extremely excited about, but think will take a substantial effort or can be held off for far in the future. The initial design, however, should take into account medium priority requirements and feasible low priority requirements such that it is simple to extend the existing system without greatly refactoring things if we decide to eventually build them. Some medium or even low priority things might make sense to ship with the initial release though depending on the implementation plan.

Additionally, I will briefly speak about the cost and storage requirements. Assume that there are no major limitations for cost or storage. Since this is a personal passion project, I do not mind spending up to $1000 building this end to end, plus minimal recurring costs to keep it up. Still, let's not make things egregiously unoptimal if we can help it.

### High Priority

Theme: building the foundation for LOTAD

1. Initial data is automatically ingested with minimal manual intervention  
   1. Beyond practical motivations, I want to start this project as a practical exploration of browser agents, and/or OpenClaw. Of course, if there is some way to do this automation without browser agents, we can just do that to keep things simple but my first thought was browser agents.  
   2. Agents should be able to, for each video in the input playlist, take all relevant information from the video title and description (see requirement 2 for “relevant information”)  
   3. Agents should be able to independently find the TouhouDB entry corresponding to each input playlist  
      1. If unable to find the corresponding TouhouDB entry, agents should surface a “task” to me to review and manually fix the DB entry. I haven’t thought too much about this task system, how it would look, or the backend, but we should have some way for human in the loop (me) to intervene when the agent encounters failures or there is incomplete data.  
      2. By the way, I noticed that on the TouhouDB website, there is already a functionality to input a youtube playlist and get back a list of matching TouhouDB pages. If there is some sort of endpoint we can hit for this that would be a huge help in filtering down the number of Youtube pages we even need to scrape.   
         1. By my estimates, when I tried importing my playlist, about 60% of the 1000+ songs were automatically recognized. Still, if I were to ingest all 10k+ songs across all playlists at that generous success rate, there would still be 4k+ songs I’d have to find by hand. That’s not to mention the scraping of those DB pages themselves, so there’s definitely still value to automating this.  
         2. TouhouDB has a github here [https://github.com/VocaDB/vocadb](https://github.com/VocaDB/vocadb) it’d be very much worth going through and understanding this. I suspect that we can reuse much of what they’ve built for this project. In fact I would recommend the agent reading this to explore this repo as the first step after reading this document.  
         3. Likewise I would also explore what’s available at [https://touhou.arrangement-chronicle.com/](https://touhou.arrangement-chronicle.com/) since they seem to be just comprehensive if not more in some aspects. Wherever I mention TouhouDB in the rest of the document you can pretty much assume I mean TouhouDB or arrangement chronicle.  
   4. Agents should reconcile the information scraped from the YT page with the entry from TouhouDB, supplementing missing information and replacing any differing information with that from TouhouDB (prioritize TouhouDB. Maybe we should do this step first and fallback to YT scraping instead of doing that for every song. Depends on how expensive YT scraping is)  
   5. Even if surfacing a task is necessary, agents should still create the corresponding entry in the DB for the video with what information is available. The one exception is if the agent comes across a song that already exists in the database (whether from the current playlist or a different one. The volume of touhou arranges is extremely large, such that I cannot reliably remember whether I already saved a song in a playlist or not). In that case, surface a task with both video links and other relevant information and then I will deduplicate them.  
   6. Playlists will contain both individual songs and (rarely) full albums or other compilation type videos. Agents should be able to gracefully handle the full album case and search for the full album instead of a song on TouhouDB when the video is an album. It should then create an entry in the DB for each song in the album unless the song already exists in the DB.  
      1. While other songs store the video link, songs that are part of an album video should probably store the video link \+ timestamp where it starts  
   7. The agent should account for potential malicious actors (Youtube uploaders that purposefully set the description with misleading / incorrect information). 99.9% will not be malicious, but there are also cases of mistakes. Trust, but verify.  
2. Songs table populated with all relevant song metadata and queryable  
   1. Song name (required)  
   2. Arranger(s)  
   3. Vocalist(s) (required for vocal songs)  
   4. Lyricist(s) (should be required for vocal songs, but in practice this field is often missing in metadata. Songs with a vocalist but no lyricist should be flagged to me for review).  
   5. Song length (required)  
      1. Does not need to be precise. If it exists in TouhouDB we should defer to that value rather than the youtube value as the uploader may introduce some extra buffer or cut out some extra parts.  
   6. Original song(s) (required)  
      1. For songs that are re-arranges of other arranges, this field should NOT be the original arrange, but rather the original ZUN song  
      2. Similarly, arranges that are arranges of ZUN songs which are themselves arranges of original ZUN songs should use the original ZUN song. For example, a song that is an arrangement of “東方妖々夢　～ Ancient Temple” from PoFV should instead be marked as an arrange of “東方妖々夢　～ Ancient Temple” from PCB because the PoFV version is itself an arrange that ZUN made of the original PCB one.  
      3. In short, if following TouhouDB conventions, for each song, we should traverse the “Original version” field to the leaf node to populate this field.  
      4. Caveat to the above, I think TouhouDB may only allow one Original version field. However, songs can have many original ZUN songs, especially in the case of medleys. In this case, the full list of originals may be listed in the description. Example below for [https://touhoudb.com/S/143853?albumId=22893](https://touhoudb.com/S/143853?albumId=22893)  
      5. There are a small handful of songs in my playlists where they are original songs or are otherwise remixed from something having nothing to do with the original Touhou or ZUN works, but I have included in here anyway because they are Touhou style arrangements, have Touhou inspired lyrics, or are otherwise Touhou-themed. In these cases you can just put “Original” in this field or whatever the original song is if it comes outside of Touhou.  
         1. Example: [https://touhoudb.com/S/25744](https://touhoudb.com/S/25744)

| Original version | [眠れる恐怖　～ Sleeping Terror](https://touhoudb.com/S/294) O ZUN |
| :---- | :---- |
| Description | Originals:[眠れる恐怖　～Sleeping Terror](http://touhoudb.com/S/294)[Dream Land](http://touhoudb.com/S/295)[アリスマエステラ](http://touhoudb.com/S/291) |

   7. Circle (technically not required because sometimes arrangers release songs not part of a circle, but only on their personal youtube/niconico/etc. However in practice almost every song should have a circle associated. We could consider making this field required and populating it with Arranger for these edge cases. Either way, missing circle should be flagged to me for review).  
   8. Album(s) (not required for same as above)  
   9. Also include the TouhouDB link and video link from the playlist (for links that are albums, also include the timestamp at which the relevant song starts)  
   10. In general, the table in our database should match or exceed TouhouDB in data quality/completeness. If there are fields that exist there, we should probably save them too (e.g language, genre, etc). But beyond the ones I listed here, the other ones are not super required.  
   11. I should be able to perform queries like:  
       1. Select all video links where the vocalist is Mei Ayakura  
       2. Get the count of songs I have saved where the circle is Sound Holic  
3. There should be a dynamic way to score each song based on what playlist it is kept in  
   1. Each playlist should be tracked alongside the metadata for each entry  
   2. For full albums, each song entry in the album should be assigned the same playlist/rating unless there are videos for individual songs in the album that are in a different playlist (e.g the album “Sally” is on REVAL but the song “min～眠～” which is on the Sally album is on playlist TOUHOU MEGAMIX). It might make sense to keep track of some source flag for each entry so if we see that the source is an album when we have a duplicate conflict, we know to just overwrite the entry instead of surfacing a task.  
   3. It should be possible to change the scores assigned to the playlists, but these scores should still be stored somewhere. For example, maybe I want to perform an analysis with “playlist 3”=5, “REVAL”=7, ”pq”=8.5, “TOUHOU MEGAMIX”=10 on a scale of 1 to 10 and another with “playlist 3” giving 1 point while “TOUHOU MEGAMIX” gives 5\.  
      1. By the way, the current playlists are “playlist 3,” “REVAL,” “pq,” and “TOUHOU MEGAMIX.” This is not expected to change, but if possible let’s design a solution that will let us support adding new playlists with different scores seamlessly.  
      2. Currently, if I don’t like a song at all it is not saved to any playlist. This could bias the analyses since “did not like” would be treated the same as “did not listen.” This could be a reason to introduce another playlist.  
   4. I should be able to perform queries like:  
      1. Which original song has the highest average score aggregated across all song entries?  
      2. Return all songs from this circle with a score greater than x  
      3. When ranking my favorite original songs by arranged scores, what are the biggest differences when compared to the results of last year’s [Touhou Vote](https://maribelhearn.com/thvote)?  
4. Support pulling “normalization metrics” from TouhouDB  
   1. The motivation behind this: suppose I have 132 arranges of “U.N. Owen was Her?” saved across my playlists and only 3 arranges of “あなたの町の怪事件” saved. However, in total, there 8000+ arranges of UN Owen and only 30 arranges of あなたの町の怪事件 (numbers all made up).  
   2. In this case, shouldn’t a proper analysis have some sort of weighting system to normalize the results?  
   3. To support this, we need to be able to pull and store a large swath of normalization metrics from TouhouDB  
      1. Count of arranges for each original song in our DB  
      2. Count of arranges for each vocalist  
      3. Count of arranges for each circle  
      4. Anything else you can think of that might be useful\!  
   4. I should be able to write queries to perform analyses like:  
      1. What is my favorite original song based on the normalized score of songs in LOTAD?  
      2. Who is my favorite artist based on the score of songs they appear on and normalized over all songs they performed?  
5. Besides the playlist score, support another separate ranking  
   1. Within each playlist, I want the ability to rank songs more finely (this really puts the O in LOTAD).  
   2. I will elaborate more on this in med or low priority section, but basically I will build some sort of sorter that allows me to compare songs manually until the relative order of each song is determined. This sorter should then be able to write these rankings to another field in the database for each song that was sorted.  
   3. For now, just make sure support for this other field exists. We don’t have to build the sorter right away.  
   4. I should be able to perform analysis like: what is my favorite original song based on a mix of playlist score and song rank?  
6. The database supports an original song to character(s) mapping  
   1. Each original theme is associated with a character or characters.  
   2. For some of them, they will be obvious because they are the character’s boss theme: ハルトマンの妖怪少女 is Koishi’s boss theme.  
   3. Other times, it’s less obvious. Last Remote is infamously debated as to whether it is a Sanae theme (because she appears as a midboss) or a Koishi theme (because she is the boss of that extra stage). In this case, just have it count toward both.  
   4. However, since Seiga Kaku is the only character to appear on her stage, both her boss theme (Old Yuanxian) and the stage theme (Desire Drive) belong to her.  
   5. Taking Hifuu albums as another example, unless the song is already associated with another character, I’d just associate it with both Renko and Maribel. Some exceptions being Innocent Treasures being more strongly associated with ZUN than either Hifuu character and Mary the Magician definitely being more Maribel’s theme than Renko’s.  
   6. This mapping should live as a separate table in our database, and each original song in the songs table should have an associated character in this table.  
      1. My current thought is that we want an original\_songs table that will also store along with associated characters, the game(s) that the song is from and potential other metadata.  
   7. Since this is a bit of an ambiguous/hairy task with room for debate, I’d propose having an LLM populate the initial table and surface tasks to me to verify anything that isn’t just cut and dry like boss themes.  
      1. This can be a future work item, but another solution would be to use YT thumbnails of associated videos to get the associated character (via some image recognition system)  
      2. The problem with this is there are malicious actors like 2hu catfish or whatever the channel is called who deliberately upload the songs with unrelated Touhou character pictures, so if we go with this approach we may need some sort of vote based system where if there are 9 vids using char A and just 1 using char B, we discard char B but if there are 3 using A and 2 using B we might say the original song is just associated with both A and B.  
   8. Thinking about this again, this is a harder problem than I initially thought. Arranges themselves may have associated characters, even if the original song doesn’t, so I wonder if we map this onto the arrange rather than the original, or if we just ignore these edge cases.  
      1. One that I can think of off the top of my head is Sanae-san, which I love using as an edge case for basically everything. It’s a Touhou song, but the original song is not Touhou. It’s an original composition. However, it’s still associated with the character Sanae, for obvious reasons.  
      2. One reason for ignoring these edge cases would be because the vast majority of songs can be handled by looking at their original songs and it would save the extra work of determining the classification on a per arrange basis  
   9. I should be able to perform analyses like:  
      1. Who is my favorite character from a music perspective (based on normalized scores of the character's original themes, if they have multiple associations then maybe average or somehow renormalize them)?  
      2. Do I like Reisen or Yuuka more based on the scores of arranges associated with their themes?  
      3. Select all Tenshi theme video links with score \>= 8 and return them ordered by score descending  
      4. Which game do I like more, Mountain of Faith or Legacy of Lunatic Kingdom based on the average score of arranges where the original themes come from those games?  
      5. Across all games, do I prefer stage 1 (stage \+ boss) themes or stage 6 themes? Can we make a graph breaking down score per stage?

### Medium Priority

Theme: making LOTAD longterm and self-maintaining

1. Keep LOTAD up to date  
   1. High pri was essentially doing the backfill for existing songs in the DB. Now we want to make sure all future changes going forward also get reflected in the DB.  
   2. Example: I listen to a song and decide that even though I previously placed it in playlist 3, I now like it enough to put it in REVAL instead. When this happens, LOTAD needs to be updated.  
   3. Unsure how this should look, could look into Youtube API to see if we can fire a webhook on playlist update. This will then query our DB via the video url which we should have saved and then apply the transition. If net new, just write the row.  
   4. Alternatively, we could have a cron or set of crons scrape the playlists periodically and update the state in the database.   
      1. This would be less realtime but I don’t necessarily need it to be super realtime. Once a day or even once a week is acceptable is what I’m thinking at least for now.  
   5. We’ll probably also want a cron to scrape new info from TouhouDB as it gets populated.  
2. Deprecate the Archiver  
   1. Currently I depend on a manual python script I run to prevent loss of music in my playlists, since the one downside of Youtube is that songs can go down forever on a moment’s notice  
   2. Script does a webscrape on the youtube playlist and diffs against a local copy of titles to find those that are missing  
   3. This way I at least know which songs should be in there and can either look for a different upload or reupload it myself  
   4. Since the DB will be the local copy and we will have some sort of sync from playlists \<\> DB via 1, the problem would largely be solved.  
   5. However, it would be nice to have it automatically launch an agent when detecting a song dropped from the playlist without transitioning to a different one, and exploring to find another upload of the song to replace it (if there are multiple it could ask me which one to select as a replacement) or automatically reupload it (more on this later)  
   6. At the very least the system should surface a Task for me to handle the dropped record whenever the regression is detected.  
3. Unify other manually maintained data stores into LOTAD  
   1. Currently I keep tabs in many places on the different ways I interact with my Touhou music listening.  
   2. The “database” for my physical collection lives here in this spreadsheet 	[The Trove](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1lKOWVTctx9-cZmtcoWtUplPzjyhIPXWrEbAXn8c71T0/edit?gid=0#gid=0)  
   3. The table of CDs I have listened to but decided to sell lives here [Available CDs](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1a5swjyukK-oTq8lGbqojMLNlrgFqdPYJqwihdbjpUEE/edit?gid=0#gid=0)  
   4. These should all live in the same database, and we should create migration jobs that can load the sheets into tables.  
   5. In parallel with this project, I am starting another project to develop a personal website where one of the main functionalities is a trivia game about obscure Touhou music trivia  
      1. These currently live as discord messages in a private server but should probably also be stored in LOTAD  
      2. The schema for this table does not need to be elaborated upon or decided as part of this project, but we should know that our database will need to support this trivia table  
      3. The other two sheets will probably also be surfaced on my webpage  
         1. Could have a storefront?  
         2. Maybe entries in The Trove can be surfaced as blog posts once I clean up the commentary yap  
         3. Frontend details unrelated to this project, don’t need to worry about the specifics but know that we will need some endpoints to read from and write to these tables as well  
   6. Additionally for physical albums, there are tracks that I like (ranging from playlist 3 to MEGAMIX) but could not find on youtube. So they are just sitting there ripped in my device’s local Music folder.  
      1. These should also be ingested into the database and some mechanism set up so that when new rips land in my music folder, the database gets synced.  
      2. Since it’s unknown what playlist I would assign them, just that I like it enough to save, a Task should be surfaced for me to approve the write to DB and assign a playlist.  
      3. Ideally we would upload these ripped songs to YT automatically, but I’m thinking that will come later (see low priority 6).  
      4. Some of these songs may have since been uploaded to YT, or even already ingested into the DB. These cases should be handled gracefully.  
4. Close the loop on the song lifecycle by automating the discovery phase  
   1. Currently how I discover Touhou music is by seeing songs that pop up in the recommended when I‘m listening on youtube, or via recommendations and song share posts on discord.  
   2. Additionally, sometimes I find a song I really like and then go on a huge spree of scouring YT and TouhouDB to find other arrangements made by that artist, circle, or sometimes even just other arranges of that theme.  
   3. Whenever I find a song, I put it into my “eval” playlist which is a queue of songs I still have to listen to. [https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLuDYUKEqeoawK20u7vX3c6HTnn3w-9oXw](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLuDYUKEqeoawK20u7vX3c6HTnn3w-9oXw)  
   4. There is also “eval-batch” mostly for albums but I don’t often use this in practice because I mostly prefer listening to songs one at a time. [https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLuDYUKEqeoax13G8ZZZeXZJYlUOkBUhvx](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLuDYUKEqeoax13G8ZZZeXZJYlUOkBUhvx)  
   5. There are a few problems with this system  
      1. Accuracy: many times the title is something weird or my limited memory forgets about a song so I enqueue a song that I already listened to and rated in the past.  
      2. Manual: discovering new songs requires me to actively pay attention to add new songs.  
      3. Coverage: the songs I can enqueue are limited to those that are in the recommended, recommended to me by friends, or related to the topic I am interested in at the time.  
   6. If this DB came with some background job that automatically scours the net and adds to the eval playlist, that would fit my workflow perfectly while solving all these problems.  
   7. Additionally, it’d be nice if there was some manual way to prompt it.  
      1. “I’ve been listening to some EdelSounds recently and really like their stuff, find some other similar songs that I haven’t listen to” and it goes and sticks em in eval  
      2. This may fit better into a low priority workflow  
   8. What this would look like in practice:  
      1. I have listened to every song in an album except one, so mark the rest as unlistened  
   9. This fully automates the discovery portion and makes it so that the only conscious effort I have to put into anything is scoring the songs.  
5. Gain valuable signal from listened but not saved songs  
   1. One large gap in our current framing is that songs that I have listened to but consciously decided not to save are treated exactly the same as songs I had no idea about or have not listened to yet.  
   2. This is acceptable for preliminary investigations which is why I didn’t put it in high priority, but there is a lot of signal being missed here.  
   3. There are a couple ways we can close this gap, and I think we should implement both.  
   4. Since we now have access to whole albums I have finished in my collection and whole albums I am selling (I will not be selling albums I have not finished), we know that any song which appears in those albums but not in any of the playlists (or local Music folder) are unsaved.  
   5. Additionally, we know that any video on YouTube that appears in my watch history but not any of the playlists (including eval) counts as unsaved.  
      1. Watch history also includes non-songs or non-Touhou songs so extra caution is needed here to prevent ingesting bad data.  
   6. These unsaved songs should be saved as full rows with their full metadata in the songs table.   
   7. When we have a full view of unsaved songs, we can begin excluding unlistened songs from the scoring queries. Or, assign those songs 0 while unsaved songs get \-1 and playlist 3 gets 1 for example.  
   8. The result will be that we get better answers for all relevant music taste queries, since we will be able to see for example, a lot of UN Owen was Her? arranges go unsaved, we should rank it lower.  
   9. We will also better deduplicate \#4 above and prevent already listened songs from coming up in the recommendations (this is basically what I do already anyway but in automated form, I don’t add to eval if I see the red bar on YouTube).  
6. Establish a framework for manual ranking of songs within playlists  
   1. Create a custom sorter like [https://tohosort.frelia.my/](https://tohosort.frelia.my/)  
   2. Rather than hardcoded Touhou characters, it should accept as input any list of songs/videos to allow them to be compared to each other  
   3. Have this sorter display on my website  
   4. Configure this sorter to take in one of the playlists from LOTAD  
   5. (optional) sorter should be configurable based on how many ranked entries you want to find, e.g if user wants top 100 and the top 100 has already been determined, we can short circuit the sorting  
   6. UI: user is presented with two songs, each with a playable video that they can click to compare. Should also work with album videos that are timestamped.  
   7. Once results are determined, they should be written back to LOTAD in the field mentioned in high priority \#5 if the source was from LOTAD  
   8. Maybe we can default to MEGAMIX on the website but let users provide their own list of songs \+ videos. We’ll probably need a defined input type that’s like a list of jsons or something for more fine tuned input while also supporting a generic playlist url input.   
      1. Only upload results to DB if the user is me  
   9. This does kind of depend on the website being up already and seems like a big lift whether it is or not, so maybe we can call this low prio if we also have to build the website from scratch.  
7. Suggest physical albums to buy  
   1. Yes, I’m putting ads in my service  
   2. This one is pretty simple, I currently maintain a list of things to look for in memory whenever there is a new sale on surugaya or the proxy I use or whatever  
   3. It’d be great if the thinking what I should get part can be automated away too  
   4. We should prioritize:   
      1. Albums that I like many of the songs on but do not physically own  
      2. Albums that are not easily accessible through YT or online Touhou music collections but the algorithm from \#4 would suggest I would enjoy  
   5. How to get this information to me is up in the air  
      1. Could be a Task to buy the suggested album, which is closed by me accepting or denying the suggestion..?  
      2. Seems weird semantically to raise this as a Task though which is primarily for reconciling data within the database. This really suggests we have to think more about the interface between the DB and the user and what Tasks are.

### Low Priority

Theme: making LOTAD the data foundation for data driven creation and the one stop shop for my Touhou music hobbies

1. Genre analysis  
   1. Genre is sometimes given by the artist on the back of the CD, other times listed in the title of a YouTube upload, or sometimes filled in by an ambitious TouhouDB user.  
   2. However, this is extremely unreliable  
      1. This data is not present in the majority of cases  
      2. Different youtubers will classify the same song as different genres, and in many cases just make up genres that don’t exist to describe the song  
      3. Bottom line: it’s extremely subjective  
   3. What if we just feed every song (the raw data/mp4) into some large AI model to classify the genre and close this data gap?  
   4. There will also need to be some hierarchy data model for genres  
      1. If someone asks me for hardcore recommendations, should I also be including hardstyle, hardtek, schranz, etc? (I picked hardcore specifically because this area of music is hard to define) If someone asks for trance recommendations, I will also have to include psytrance, regular trance, uplifting trance, etc?  
      2. Thankfully this problem is mostly solved already and TouhouDB has a model we can copy paste to start with  
   5. Results:  
      1. Should be able to perform queries to find my favorite genre(s) based on rating  
      2. Should be able to perform queries to create recommendation playlists for anyone that asks for a certain genre (broad or specific)  
2. Lyrics  
   1. Here is a huge painpoint: I go to sing a song in karaoke only to find that there are no lyrics for that song. Then I painstakingly manually transcribe the lyrics, from the booklet if I happen to have the CD, translated if it is on the Chinese Touhou wiki, or worst case by ear.  
   2. We should ideally store lyrics (original, translated, romaji) whenever they are available. This will help not only for me but also for \#8 below.  
      1. Scour the web for lyrics and save them to the database. The primary sources will be thwikicc (chinese wiki), touhou wiki (english), and translation pages (releska, amen, etc).  
   3. Beyond this, we should also have a large AI model for parsing out lyrics when unavailable to get complete coverage.  
   4. This gives an added bonus of accurate language determination (the language tag is functionally dead on TouhouDB).  
3. Google API integration for custom playlist sorting and creation  
   1. When I first started my Touhou music journey, I had a small playlist of 300 songs that I religiously kept sorted and up to date as I inserted new songs.   
   2. Alstroemeria records would have to be next to shibayan records, all the stack songs would be next to each other, more hype stuff at the beginning more chill stuff at the end etc  
   3. However this behavior proved unscalable very quickly as YouTube’s awful custom sorting UI where you had to manually drag and drop the videos where you wanted them to go, which sometimes did not even work as intended, made it so that all inserts became O(n).   
   4. Eventually I gave up on this habit and began shuffle playing every time anyway, but hopefully it does not have to be this way\!  
   5. At this point in the project, if we have not yet integrated with Google API, it would be time to explore whether or not it would be possible to use it and fiddle some way to solve this issue  
   6. Assuming it’s possible, we should be able to create any arbitrary order within the database (or sequence of least costly moves to get to said arbitrary order, if that’s what Google API accepts) and send it over to reflect that ordering within the existing playlist. Of course, this ordering should also be persisted within the songs table.  
   7. In conjunction with \#7, this will allow us to keep our playlist(s) in some sort of logical sorted order, if so desired.  
   8. Semi-related so I didn’t feel like breaking it into its own section, we should also explore if YouTube API allows us to better clear up/recover deleted videos and prevent them from clogging up space in our playlists. Currently it’s really hard to see deleted videos and remove them let alone investigate them without the help of Archiver, there is a button on playlists that lets you show unavailable videos but somehow this does not always pop up even when there are definitely unavailable videos within the playlist (REVAL currently has 40, used to be way more). If the API lets us programmatically address these cases that would be great.  
4. LastFM integration  
   1. [LastFM](https://www.last.fm/user/animon22) is ridiculously terrible at parsing the songs I am currently listening to, see: screenshot where the artist and song name are frequently swapped, or song name gets parsed into both fields, or worse  
      1. ![][image1]  
      2. It’d be one thing if it just swapped artist and name but it’s even worse than that, if I had to guess its reliability is maybe about 10% which is far worse than random chance  
   2. I have a load of other problems with lastFM but unfortunately it’s what everyone uses and if people want a quick glance into listening history, I better keep it up to date.  
   3. Since the DB has the source of truth, I wonder if it would be possible to set up some kind of a hook that when I listen to a song on YT or YT music \-\> calls into DB \-\> gets canonical artist information \-\> sends it over to LastFM. This would cover all new listens where they are on an existing playlist, but not the case where it’s a net new song (that isn’t on eval). That is fine though since my flow for exploration is basically 100% off of eval.  
   4. Additionally, for paid members I think LastFM has an option to clean up historical scrobbles (which are pretty much all garbage right now). If we can actually get this integration working, I wouldn’t mind paying a temporary subscription and just have some script best guess the actual song that LastFM was looking up.  
      1. Search DB for matches with artist name, partial song name, Youtube channel uploader, etc.  
   5. Additionally, I think it’d be fun as an extra thing to explore integration with Discord. While the current song is already displayed on LastFM, I think it’d also be cool to dynamically set my Discord status based on the song I’m listening to as another feature in the live update genre of improvements.  
5. TouhouDB / Thwiki reciprocal value  
   1. If we really do build out a lot of these low prio improvements (lyric parsing, genre classification, song clustering etc) I can see a world where we are not only ingesting data from these sources like TouhouDB, but actually providing value back.  
   2. Even without these improvements, just by nature of me owning a bunch of albums and finding YT vids that the TouhouDB admin is not even aware of and these getting surfaced as Tasks, we’ll be in a position to bridge gaps in existing data sources.  
   3. This part of the project, if we decide to do it, should be about building scripts and automations that can sync new discoveries from our workflows, for example missing lyrics on the chinese touhou wiki, and have some way to automatically pipe that data through.  
6. Youtube auto uploads  
   1. As previously mentioned, I have the local folder of ripped Touhou music for uploads I couldn’t find on YouTube. Ideally, I’d upload all of these myself and put them into the appropriate playlist.  
   2. However, uploading a video takes a lot of time and effort. I have a high bar for my videos, they have to have the most appropriate background image chiefly, a good visualization, title and description properly filled out, animations as appropriate, and lyrics ideally within the video itself or otherwise just in the description. I still have a fulltime job so maintaining uploads for the hundreds in my backlog folder is just not realistic.  
   3. Ideally, some agent will find the most appropriate image (the most tedious part, oftentimes it’s just the album image though especially if the theme is one of the character in the art), parse out the lyrics, do the intro outro animation and add the lyrics in sync to the singing, then add some sort of visualizer on top. Currently I use [vizzy.io](http://vizzy.io) but I don’t know if there’s something more easily used by AI agents.   
   4. Then upload the video on my second channel [https://www.youtube.com/@KeineKamishirasawa430](https://www.youtube.com/@KeineKamishirasawa430) as unlisted and simultaneously drop it into eval. Then I’d listen through it during the exploration phase to give a classification of rating and decide if it should go public (some artists really don’t want to have their stuff on YT, we want to avoid getting the channel striked down. Right now most videos are unlisted just because of all the strikes and this is fine for appeasing those artists).  
7. Song clustering and representative song discovery  
   1. Let’s go back to the playlist sorting example to introduce this idea.  
   2. How would we determine a good sorting? Previously I just went on vibes. Sure we could go by artist, but then how do you determine which artists should be next to each other? And once you line up circle A \-\> circle B \-\> circle C, how do you determine which songs from circle B are most similar to those of circles A and C respectively? Can you sort them on a spectrum from similarity to A to C?  
   3. What we really want is some sort of song clustering that takes in all data about a song, including its raw file bytes, and places each song on some sort of higher dimensional grid where there is a semblance of distance between songs, which can further be aggregated into artists.  
   4. This stands out to me as one of the most useful things we could do in the low priority category simply because beyond the song sorting example, it unlocks so many avenues of analysis.  
   5. Another example, let’s say I recently listened to a South of Heaven song and really liked what I heard, and now I want to find other songs/circles similar to that one. Normally to do this I’d need to ask my friend with esoteric knowledge of metal circles. And normally for most things, I am that esoteric friend… with song clustering, I’ll be able to answer these questions myself and go beyond the limitations of my own knowledge.  
   6. Another cool idea, suggested rises and drops. Say in REVAL there is a large cluster of songs that all share some similar characteristics and thus are clustered together. We have some automation that detects there is a different song in playlist 3 that is also clustered close to these songs, so maybe we surface this song somehow (whether that’s by adjusting its weight if \#9 is implemented, as a Task, or sticking it in some other playlist) for re-review. Essentially saying, hey you rated this song this way in the past, but it is now more similar to songs in this tier, maybe your taste has changed.  
   7. We will want some way to say that after we have re-reviewed though, it does not keep spamming us with that same suggestion if we decided it should stay.  
   8. More generally song clustering could be used to improve song suggestion and the eval loop since we’re now operating on a more complete set of data.  
8. Song data collection for generative AI training  
   1. These last two ideas are by far the ones I am most excited about and the most out there.  
   2. If we are already ingesting raw song files as pure data in the above, why not use this data to train or fine tune an AI?  
   3. I’ll need to read more on the literature to see what’s SotA and how exactly we can do this but my end goal is basically to create [Suno AI](https://suno.com/home) but finetuned for Touhou music generation according to my specific tastes.  
      1. I won’t even claim to know how Suno operates under the hood or if this even works, but when I was in school we could use examples to fine tune existing models to achieve a more targeted output (e.g feed ZUN art examples into stable diffusion and generate Touhou characters). I am not familiar with how the landscape has changed since LLMs.  
      2. Take in detailed text input on what the song should be  
      3. Outputs a MIDI or whatever output format people use for digital music creation, I won’t claim to know these things for now  
   4. Since I have already “labeled” the data by ordering the songs, we can simply weigh songs in each playlist accordingly. Example: train on each song in MEGAMIX 5 times, each song in pq 4 times, each song in playlist 3 1 time.  
   5. I’d want it outputted as a MIDI so I can actually “inspect” the song myself and learn alongside generating songs through the AI  
      1. Fitting to some strict model with its own separate components (separate vocal track, separate instruments that play at set intervals) rather than having it generate generic audio will also (hopefully) prevent it from generating pure arbitrary garbage that has no limitations on how it can blend together.  
      2. However, this does mean that I will have to do an even more (seemingly large) amount of work to convert each song’s raw audio file into the desired output format for training. Additionally, I’d have to concatenate all the metadata somehow so that it knows when I request a Hartmanns youkai girl arrange which training samples that is most similar to.  
      3. Additionally, maybe I will ask it to find me a synthesizer most similar to Mei Ayakura (or whatever vocalist I feel like will most fit the song) and use that for the track, so the model should support this. I don’t think I would feel comfortable having it actually generate songs using the voice of the person it trained on but synths should be fine.  
   6. Since the songs holistically reflect my taste, I’m hoping the chatbot can point out certain things as I’m requesting songs. For example, maybe it notices that I really like some certain sound effect or chord progression (obviously I don’t know music theory or most of the proper terms for this stuff) and points out where in the song it generated it included that.  
      1. It’s basically the benefits of the clustering step above in \#7, but made explicit and presented in a way that’s instructive. These songs are similar, here’s \*why\* they’re similar, why you like it, and how you can incorporate that in your music.  
   7. Throughout this process of generating songs and learning from the AI, I’m hoping to learn about digital music arrangement and music theory on the fly, eventually to the point that I’m competent enough to do it on my own.  
   8. But, the model we build will be there to deliver a personalized experience and ensure that from the get-go, I am delivering creative output that I am at least somewhat satisfied with.  
9. Mini gensokyo radio / rensokyo gadio  
   1. Here’s a big problem:  
      1. The framework is setup so that when I listen to a song I saved in say REVAL, I get to decide whether it stays in REVAL, goes up to pq, or goes down to playlist 3\. That is why it’s called REVAL, so I can re-evaluate it.  
      2. However, in practice once I already determined that I like these songs the best wouldn’t I always be listening to TOUHOU MEGAMIX?  
      3. To get around this, I will force myself occasionally to go through REVAL or pq instead of my usual so I can actually see if my rating of any songs changed.  
   2. Instead, what if we reinvent Gensokyo Radio? We can even call it Rensokyo Gadio.  
   3. The idea would be to have adjustable weights for each playlist (we can even include eval) and when the radio decides what song to play, it will first select based on the playlist weight and then the song within the playlist.  
   4. Example: we only enable this for TOUHOU MEGAMIX and REVAL. Megamix is given a weight of 0.8 and REVAL is given a weight of 0.2. Say there are 1k songs in megamix and 3k in reval. Then, each song has a 0.8\*1/1000 chance of being selected if in megamix or a 0.2\*1/3000 chance of being selected if in REVAL.  
   5. We can call these configurations ‘experiments’ or ‘weights’. Experiments would allow us to solve the “playlist 3 is never listened to” problem: we can just have an experiment that weighs playlist 3 in at 0.1 or something while half the time we still get served megamix.  
   6. This also adds in a comparison benefit: maybe I feel like that playlist 3 song is just as good as the REVAL song that got served before it, so that gives me signal to raise the rating.  
   7. This also provides an easy way to ‘sample my taste’ to friends without just defaulting to handing them MEGAMIX.  
   8. The radio can also be hosted on my website or maybe to start out simple just create Youtube playlists via the Youtube API for each session.  
   9. Sooner rather than later though, we should provide an interface with some buttons to allow me to raise or lower the playlist in just one click.  
   10. Some consideration: maybe some sort of duplicated global rating system so that I have my private scores, but “users” of RG can still weigh in on how often songs get played?  
       1. E.g Fall in the Dark starts at MEGAMIX, Desert Years starts at REVAL, Myon6 starts at p3. I decide Desert Years should be moved up to MEGAMIX so I send it up. But a couple users both decide Desert Years should be moved down. So the end result is it gets played as often as Myon7 (one move up, two moves down), but internally, it’s still reflected as being in the MEGAMIX playlist.  
10. Multi-user support  
    1. Continuing the theme of above, if possible, it’d be good to design our systems to be valuable to other users  
    2. Anyone should be able to download the github repo and spin up a database for their ordered music   
    3. They may have different playlists, different names, rating systems, maybe it’s not even all Touhou music  
    4. However, I think this can be considered the “lowest priority” of all requirements and is really more of a nice to have. Everything we work on should be catered primarily to my workflows.

## Super Simplified Architecture Diagram

After writing down all the requirements above, it has occurred to me that what we want is really not only a database, but some sort of “service” that wraps the database and is able to programatically interact with external entities. Here is a simplified map of how I envision these systems interacting, summarizing the end state of all requirements above being satisfied.  
![][image2]  
Legend: color of the connections reflects where those connections are described in the requirements.

* Black: high priority  
* Green: medium priority  
* Pink: low priority


## Schema

All tables are defined in `lotad/db/models.py` and created by `alembic/versions/0001_initial_schema.py`. The migration is the source of truth for column types; this section documents intent, relationships, and design rationale.

### Enum types (PostgreSQL custom types)

| Enum | Values |
|------|--------|
| `media_type` | `GAME`, `MUSIC_CD`, `BOOK`, `OTHER` |
| `artist_type` | `CIRCLE`, `INDIVIDUAL`, `UNIT`, `LABEL`, `VOCALIST` |
| `song_role` | `ARRANGER`, `VOCALIST`, `LYRICIST`, `COMPOSER`, `INSTRUMENTALIST`, `MIXER`, `MASTERING`, `CHORUS` |
| `language` | `JAPANESE`, `ENGLISH`, `CHINESE`, `GERMAN`, `KOREAN`, `FRENCH`, `INSTRUMENTAL`, `OTHER` |
| `appearance_type` | `PLAYABLE`, `BOSS`, `MIDBOSS`, `STAGE_ENEMY`, `SUPPORTING`, `MENTIONED` |
| `confidence_level` | `HIGH`, `MEDIUM`, `LOW` |
| `source_type` | `INDIVIDUAL_VIDEO`, `COMPOSITE_VIDEO` — individual = one song per video; composite = multiple songs in one video (full albums, medleys, back-to-back tracks) |
| `task_status` | `OPEN`, `IN_PROGRESS`, `RESOLVED`, `DISMISSED` |
| `task_type` | `FILL_MISSING_INFO`, `DEDUPLICATE_SONGS`, `REVIEW_ALBUM_TRACKS`, `ASSIGN_PLAYLIST`, `REVIEW_CHARACTER_MAPPING`, `MISSING_LYRICIST`, `MISSING_CIRCLE`, `SUSPICIOUS_METADATA`, `DROPPED_VIDEO`, `REVIEW_LOCAL_TRACK`, `INGEST_FAILED`, `TOUHOUDB_UNREACHABLE` |
| `normalization_entity_type` | `ORIGINAL_SONG`, `ARTIST`, `CIRCLE` |
| `physical_album_status` | `OWNED`, `SOLD`, `WISHLIST` |
| `SongType` | `ARRANGEMENT`, `REARRANGEMENT`, `REMIX`, `COVER`, `MASHUP`, `INSTRUMENTAL`, `ORIGINAL`, `REMASTER`, `LIVE`, `SHORT_VERSION`, `OTHER` — mirrors TouhouDB's SongType. Most LOTAD entries are `ARRANGEMENT`. `REARRANGEMENT` is an arrangement of an existing arrangement. `INSTRUMENTAL` means an instrumental version exists for a vocal song. `ORIGINAL` for non-Touhou originals. |
| `DiscType` | `ALBUM`, `SINGLE`, `EP`, `SPLIT`, `COMPILATION`, `GAME`, `FANMADE`, `INSTRUMENTAL`, `VIDEO`, `OTHER` — mirrors TouhouDB's DiscType. `GAME` for ZUN's official game OSTs; `FANMADE` for unofficial fan game OSTs. |

### Core reference tables

#### `works`
Canonical Touhou games, ZUN music CDs, and print works (manga/books). Seeded from `lotad/db/seeds/works.py`.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `name` | text | no | Full English title |
| `short_name` | text | yes | Abbreviation, e.g. `PCB`, `IN` |
| `media_type` | media_type | no | `GAME`, `MUSIC_CD`, `BOOK`, `OTHER` |
| `release_year` | integer | yes | |
| `canonical_order` | numeric(5,1) | yes | Decimal to allow `.5` (fighters/spin-offs), null for CDs/books |
| `notes` | text | yes | e.g. "Fighter; co-developed with Twilight Frontier" |

#### `original_songs`
ZUN's original compositions. Each row is one track from a game OST or ZUN music CD. This is the leaf node referred to in song arrangement chains — recursive `original_version` traversal on TouhouDB should resolve here.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `touhoudb_id` | integer | yes | unique; used for dedup on re-import |
| `name` | text | no | Japanese title |
| `name_romanized` | text | yes | |
| `work_id` | integer FK → works | no | |
| `stage` | integer | yes | Stage number within the game |
| `track_number` | integer | yes | OST track number |
| `is_boss` | boolean | no | default false |
| `is_extra_stage` | boolean | no | default false |
| `notes` | text | yes | |

#### `characters`
Touhou characters. `first_appearance_work_id` allows queries like "all characters from Mountain of Faith." Also covers non-game characters (e.g. Watatsuki sisters from SSiB manga, Akyuu from PMiSS).

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `name` | text | no | Japanese name |
| `name_romanized` | text | yes | |
| `first_appearance_work_id` | integer FK → works | yes | Null for characters with ambiguous first appearance |
| `is_playable` | boolean | no | default false; true for protagonists |
| `notes` | text | yes | |

#### `artists`
All named contributors — circles, individual arrangers, vocalists, lyricists. **Circles are also rows in this table** with `artist_type = CIRCLE`; the `artist_circles` join table links individuals to their circle(s). This avoids a separate `circles` table and handles multi-circle artists cleanly.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `touhoudb_id` | integer | yes | unique |
| `name` | text | no | |
| `name_romanized` | text | yes | |
| `artist_type` | artist_type | no | `CIRCLE`, `INDIVIDUAL`, `UNIT` |
| `touhoudb_url` | text | yes | |

#### `albums`
Doujin/commercial albums. An album has zero or more circles (via `album_circles`) and zero or more events (via `album_events`).

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `touhoudb_id` | integer | yes | unique |
| `title` | text | no | |
| `title_romanized` | text | yes | |
| `release_date` | date | yes | |
| `catalog_number` | text | yes | |
| `touhoudb_url` | text | yes | |
| `disc_type` | DiscType | no | default ALBUM |
| `description` | text | yes | |
| `barcode` | text | yes | |

#### `songs`
The core entity. One row = one arrangement. May appear in multiple playlists (via `playlist_songs`) and on multiple albums (via `album_tracks`). `is_original_composition = true` for songs that are not Touhou arrangements (e.g. Sanae-san).

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `touhoudb_id` | integer | yes | unique |
| `title` | text | no | |
| `title_romanized` | text | yes | |
| `duration_seconds` | integer | yes | Prefer TouhouDB value over YouTube |
| `touhoudb_url` | text | yes | |
| `arrangement_chronicle_url` | text | yes | |
| `has_lyrics` | boolean | no | default false |
| `is_original_composition` | boolean | no | default false; true for non-ZUN originals |
| `created_at` | timestamptz | no | |
| `updated_at` | timestamptz | no | |
| `notes` | text | yes | |
| `song_type` | SongType | no | default ARRANGEMENT |
| `publish_date` | date | yes | Official release date (may differ from album release_date) |
| `min_milli_bpm` | integer | yes | BPM stored as millibpm (e.g. 174000 = 174 BPM); mirrors TouhouDB |
| `max_milli_bpm` | integer | yes | Upper bound for variable-tempo songs; equals min_milli_bpm for fixed tempo |

#### `youtube_videos`
Tracks a single YouTube video (11-char ID). A video may be linked from multiple songs (album video with timestamps) or playlists. `is_available` goes false when 404/410 is detected during sync. `last_checked_at` is updated every sync run; `updated_at` only changes when a data field changes.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `video_id` | text | no | unique; YouTube 11-char ID |
| `title` | text | yes | YouTube title at time of last check |
| `channel_id` | text | yes | |
| `channel_name` | text | yes | |
| `description` | text | yes | Raw YouTube description |
| `duration_seconds` | integer | yes | |
| `is_available` | boolean | no | default true |
| `last_checked_at` | timestamptz | yes | Updated every sync run |
| `updated_at` | timestamptz | no | Updated only on data change |

#### `playlists`
The five playlists seeded at startup. `display_order` encodes tier (1 = highest quality).

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `name` | text | no | e.g. `TOUHOU MEGAMIX`, `pq`, `REVAL`, `eval`, `playlist 3` |
| `youtube_playlist_id` | text | no | unique; the list= query string value |
| `display_order` | integer | no | 1–5 ascending; 1 = best tier |

#### `scoring_configurations`
Named weight maps keyed by playlist name. `is_default = true` on exactly one row. `weights` is JSON mapping playlist name to a numeric weight. Three configs are seeded:

| Config | Semantic | Example weights |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| `default` | Additive/frequency model — points per playlist tier | MEGAMIX=10, pq=7, REVAL=4, playlist 3=1, eval=0 |
| `ten_point` | Rating model — playlist tier as score out of 10 | MEGAMIX=10, pq=8.5, REVAL=7, playlist 3=5, eval=0 |
| `equal` | All evaluated playlists equal weight | MEGAMIX=pq=REVAL=playlist 3=1, eval=0 |

Future: weights should be tweakable without code changes — a config store or env-level override so the user can experiment with logarithmic/exponential scales (not yet implemented; currently hard-coded in the seed script).

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `name` | text | no | unique; e.g. `default`, `equal` |
| `description` | text | yes | |
| `weights` | JSON | no | Map of playlist name → numeric weight |
| `is_default` | boolean | no | default false |
| `created_at` | timestamptz | no | |

#### `tasks`
Human-in-the-loop review queue. `data` is a JSONB payload whose shape varies by `task_type`. Tasks are never blocking — queries run against partial data but the `lotad tasks` dashboard shows outstanding items.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `task_type` | task_type | no | see enum above |
| `status` | task_status | no | default `OPEN` |
| `priority` | integer | no | default 5; lower = more urgent |
| `title` | text | no | Human-readable summary |
| `data` | JSON | no | Type-specific payload, default `{}` |
| `related_song_id` | integer FK → songs | yes | |
| `related_video_id` | integer FK → youtube_videos | yes | |
| `created_at` | timestamptz | no | |
| `resolved_at` | timestamptz | yes | Set when status → RESOLVED/DISMISSED |
| `auto_created_by` | text | yes | Pipeline step that created the task |

#### `normalization_metrics`
Stores the total arrangement count for a given entity fetched from TouhouDB. Used to normalize scores (e.g. a song with only 30 total arranges getting 5 of them in MEGAMIX is more impressive than UN Owen getting 200/8000). Refreshed on-demand with a 24h TTL.

| Column | Type | Nullable | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| `id` | integer PK | — | |
| `entity_type` | normalization_entity_type | no | `ORIGINAL_SONG`, `ARTIST`, `CIRCLE` |
| `entity_id` | integer | no | FK into `original_songs`, `artists` respectively |
| `total_arrange_count` | integer | no | |
| `last_fetched_at` | timestamptz | no | |
| Unique | (`entity_type`, `entity_id`) | — | |

#### `physical_albums` and `physical_tracks`
Tracks physical CD collection. `physical_albums` may or may not be linked to an `albums` row (unmatched physical CDs get a task). `physical_tracks` may or may not link to a `songs` row.

`physical_album_status` values: `OWNED` (in collection), `SOLD` (historical), `WISHLIST` (target to buy).

### Join tables

| Table | PK / Unique | Purpose |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| `original_song_characters` | (`original_song_id`, `character_id`) | Maps original ZUN songs to associated Touhou characters. `confidence` = HIGH for boss themes, MEDIUM/LOW for ambiguous assignments. |
| `character_works` | (`character_id`, `work_id`, `appearance_type`) | A character's appearances across works. Multiple rows per character if they appear in multiple capacities (e.g. playable + boss). |
| `artist_circles` | (`artist_id`, `circle_id`) | Links individual artists to circles. `is_primary = true` for their main circle. Both IDs reference `artists`. |
| `album_circles` | (`album_id`, `circle_id`) | An album's releasing circles. `is_primary = true` for the main circle. |
| `album_events` | (`album_id`, `event_name`) | Conventions/events where an album was released (e.g. `C103`, `M3-2023`). |
| `album_tracks` | unique (`album_id`, `disc_number`, `track_number`) | Song's position on an album. Optional `youtube_video_id` + `youtube_timestamp_seconds` for album-video links. |
| `song_artists` | (`song_id`, `artist_id`, `role`) | Contributors to a song by role (ARRANGER, VOCALIST, LYRICIST, COMPOSER). A person can have multiple roles on one song. |
| `song_languages` | (`song_id`, `language`) | Languages present in a song. Instrumental songs get `INSTRUMENTAL`. |
| `song_originals` | (`song_id`, `original_song_id`) | Original ZUN song(s) being arranged. Medleys get multiple rows. |
| `song_characters` | (`song_id`, `character_id`) | Characters associated with an *arrangement* specifically (for original compositions like Sanae-san, or character-focused arranges not reflected by the original song). |
| `playlist_songs` | unique (`song_id`, `playlist_id`, `removed_at`) | A song's presence in a playlist at a point in time. `removed_at` is null for current entries. `source_type` distinguishes individual video vs. album video. `rank` is the fine-grained user rank within the playlist. |
| `song_tags` | (`song_id`, `tag` text, `count` int) | Freeform genre/mood tags sourced from TouhouDB; `count` reflects tag vote weight. |
| `album_tags` | (`album_id`, `tag` text, `count` int) | Same as song_tags but for albums. |

### Indexes

Beyond primary keys and unique constraints, these indexes are pre-created in M1 for expected query patterns:

| Index | Table | Columns |
|-------|-------|---------|
| `ix_playlist_songs_playlist_id` | playlist_songs | `playlist_id` |
| `ix_playlist_songs_song_id` | playlist_songs | `song_id` |
| `ix_playlist_songs_removed_at` | playlist_songs | `removed_at` |
| `ix_tasks_status` | tasks | `status` |
| `ix_tasks_task_type` | tasks | `task_type` |
| `ix_song_originals_original_song_id` | song_originals | `original_song_id` |
| `ix_normalization_metrics_entity_type_entity_id` | normalization_metrics | `entity_type`, `entity_id` |

## Implementation

### Getting Started (M0 Checklist)

Before running any LOTAD code, complete these one-time setup steps:

1. **Clone the repo and install dependencies**
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/AM22/lotad.git
   cd lotad
   uv sync --extra dev   # installs all Python deps incl. dev tools
   ```

2. **Create your `.env` file** from the template:
   ```bash
   cp .env.example .env
   ```
   Then fill in:
   - `DATABASE_URL` — Supabase Transaction Pooler connection string (Project Settings → Database → Connection pooling → Transaction mode). Format: `postgresql+psycopg://[user]:[password]@[host]:6543/postgres`
   - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — from https://console.anthropic.com
   - `YOUTUBE_API_KEY` — from Google Cloud Console (YouTube Data API v3 enabled)
   - Optionally override `ANTHROPIC_MODEL`, `TOUHOUDB_REQUEST_TIMEOUT`, etc.

3. **Run the first migration** to create all tables:
   ```bash
   uv run alembic upgrade head
   ```

4. **Seed the initial data:**
   ```bash
   uv run lotad db seed-all
   # Seeds: works (games + music CDs + books), playlists, scoring configs
   ```

5. **Verify the smoke tests pass:**
   ```bash
   uv run pytest -v
   ```

6. **Install Playwright browser** (required from M4 onward):
   ```bash
   uv run playwright install chromium
   ```

7. **Set up Windows Task Scheduler** (required from M5 onward): Create a task pointing to `uv run lotad sync --all` with trigger "Daily at 02:00" and the "Run task as soon as possible after a scheduled start is missed" checkbox ticked. This catches up on the next boot if the machine was off at 02:00.

---

### Design Philosophy

**Default to lists.** Every field that could conceivably have multiple values uses a join table rather than a scalar FK. The Touhou doujin space is large and decentralized — there will always be edge cases: a song with three lyricists, an album co-released by two circles, a vocalist who belongs to multiple circles across their career. Designing for lists up front prevents future migrations.

---

### Milestones

Each bullet below corresponds to roughly one implementable unit of work (think: a JIRA ticket or a PR). Dependencies between bullets are listed where relevant.

---

#### Milestone 0 — Project Scaffolding *(Complete)*

- [x] Initialize `uv` project with `pyproject.toml` (all prod + dev deps, ruff + pytest + mypy config, `lotad` CLI entry point)
- [x] Create `lotad/{db,ingestion,agents,tasks,cli,sync}/__init__.py` package skeleton
- [x] Implement `lotad/config.py` — `pydantic-settings` `Settings` class reading from `.env`; `@lru_cache` singleton; all tuneable constants (timeouts, retries, circuit breaker threshold, normalization TTL, checkpoint path)
- [x] Implement `lotad/db/session.py` — `get_engine()` with `pool_pre_ping=True`; `get_session_factory()`
- [x] Initialize Alembic with `alembic init alembic`; update `alembic/env.py` to read `DATABASE_URL` from settings; update `alembic/script.py.mako`
- [x] Create stub CLI groups: `lotad ingest`, `lotad tasks`, `lotad score`, `lotad sync`; wire into `lotad/cli/main.py`
- [x] Create `.env.example` documenting all required and optional variables
- [x] Create `.gitignore` (Python standard + `.env` + `__pycache__`)
- [x] Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — lint (ruff check + format check) + test (pytest) jobs; SQLite fallback in CI
- [x] Create `tests/test_smoke.py` — package importable, CLI importable, config importable

---

#### Milestone 1 — Core Database Schema *(Complete)*

- [x] Define all 11 enum types as `enum.StrEnum` subclasses in `lotad/db/models.py`
- [x] Define all 24 SQLAlchemy Core `Table` objects with explicit FK constraint names following the `MetaData` naming convention
- [x] Define 7 query-pattern indexes (playlist_songs, tasks, song_originals, normalization_metrics)
- [x] Write `alembic/versions/0001_initial_schema.py` — create/drop all enum types + all tables in FK-dependency order; reversible `downgrade()`
- [x] Write `lotad/db/seeds/works.py` — seed 32 games (PC-98 + Windows including TH20), 12 ZUN music CDs, 13 print works; idempotent
- [x] Write `lotad/db/seeds/playlists.py` — seed 5 playlists with real YouTube IDs; seed `default` and `equal` scoring configurations
- [x] Add `lotad db` CLI group with `seed-works`, `seed-playlists`, `seed-all` commands

---

#### Milestone 2 — TouhouDB Integration Layer *(Complete)*

**HTTP client foundation**
- [x] Implement `lotad/ingestion/http_client.py` — `httpx.AsyncClient` with `hishel` RFC-7234 cache (disk-backed, configurable TTL), base URL config, `User-Agent` header
- [x] Implement `tenacity` retry decorator with exponential backoff: initial 1s, multiplier 2, max 30s, max 5 attempts; reraise on 4xx (not retryable)
- [x] Implement circuit breaker: `CircuitBreaker` class that counts consecutive failures; after threshold (default 10) sets `is_open = True`; all subsequent calls raise `CircuitBreakerOpen` without hitting the network; resets on successful call or explicit reset

**TouhouDB API client (`lotad/ingestion/touhoudb_client.py`)**
- [x] `lookup_by_youtube_url(url: str) -> SongDetail | None` — hits `GET /api/songs/byPv` with pvService + pvId; returns None on 404
- [x] `get_song(song_id: int) -> SongDetail` — `GET /api/songs/{id}` with full field expansion; Pydantic model for response
- [x] `get_album(album_id: int) -> AlbumDetail` — `GET /api/albums/{id}`; includes tracklist
- [x] `get_artist(artist_id: int) -> ArtistDetail` — `GET /api/artists/{id}`
- [x] `resolve_original_chain(song_id: int) -> list[int]` — recursively follows `originalVersionId` to leaf; cycle detection via visited set; scans penultimate node's notes + webLinks for extra `touhoudb.com/S/<id>` refs to handle medleys; max depth 10
- [x] `get_normalization_count(entity_type: str, entity_id: int) -> int` — returns arrangement count for an original song / artist / circle
- [ ] Add integration tests (skip if `DATABASE_URL` is SQLite in CI): one happy-path test per method against the live TouhouDB API, skipped with `pytest.mark.skipif` *(partial — smoke tests exist but full per-method coverage pending)*

**Data mappers**
- [x] `map_song_detail_to_db(detail: SongDetail, conn) -> int` — upsert into songs + song_artists + song_languages + song_originals + song_characters; return song_id
- [x] `map_album_detail_to_db(detail: AlbumDetail, conn) -> int` — upsert into albums + album_circles + album_events + album_tracks; return album_id

---

#### Milestone 3 — Core Ingestion Pipeline *(Complete)*

**YouTube playlist reader**
- [x] Implement `lotad/ingestion/youtube_client.py` — `GoogleAPIClient` wrapping `google-api-python-client`; `list_playlist_items(playlist_id: str) -> AsyncIterator[PlaylistItem]` with pagination
- [x] Implement `PlaylistItem` Pydantic model: `video_id`, `title`, `description`, `channel_id`, `channel_name`, `duration_seconds`, `position`
- [x] Implement progress checkpoint: save last processed `position` + `video_id` to `settings.ingestion_checkpoint_path` after each successful batch of 10; `--resume` flag loads checkpoint on startup

**Album detection**
- [x] Implement `is_album_video(item: PlaylistItem) -> bool` — heuristic: duration > 20 min OR title contains album indicator patterns (`M3`, `C\d+`, `full album`, etc.)
- [x] Implement `extract_timestamps(description: str) -> list[tuple[int, str]]` — regex-parse `MM:SS` / `HH:MM:SS` + track title patterns from YouTube description; return `[(seconds, title), ...]`

**TouhouDB-first ingestion path (covers ~60% of videos)**
- [x] `ingest_single_video(item: PlaylistItem, playlist_id: int, conn)` — main dispatch function; calls TouhouDB lookup first; falls back to LLM path (M4) if no match
- [x] Upsert `youtube_videos` row on every call regardless of match status
- [x] On TouhouDB match: call `map_song_detail_to_db`; create `playlist_songs` row; create `album_tracks` row if album video; handle album video: call `map_album_detail_to_db`, create per-song `album_tracks` rows with timestamps
- [x] Deduplication check: before inserting `playlist_songs`, check if `song_id` already exists in any playlist; if so, create `DEDUPLICATE_SONGS` task and continue (do not skip the write — create the row with `source_type` flagged, let task resolution handle cleanup)
- [x] Source-flag conflict resolution: if existing row has `source_type = COMPOSITE_VIDEO` and new is `INDIVIDUAL_VIDEO`, overwrite silently (individual video is a more specific match); reverse case surfaces task

**CLI**
- [x] Implement `lotad ingest playlist <youtube-playlist-id>` — invokes pipeline for all videos in playlist; `--resume` flag; `--limit N` for testing
- [x] Implement `lotad ingest video <youtube-video-id>` — ingest single video (useful for testing and manual adds)
- [x] Rich progress bar via `rich.progress` showing current video title + % complete + ETA
- [x] Per-video error isolation: catch and log exceptions per video; do not abort the whole run on a single failure; increment `INGEST_FAILED` task counter and continue

**Integration test**
- [ ] Test `lotad ingest playlist <MEGAMIX_ID> --limit 20` against a real Supabase DB (skipped in CI); verify no crash and ≥1 song row inserted *(pending)*

---

#### Milestone 4 — LLM Fallback Path + Task System *(Partially Complete)*

**Arrangement Chronicle scraper**
- [ ] Implement `lotad/agents/arrangement_chronicle.py` — OpenClaw agent that searches `https://touhou.arrangement-chronicle.com/detail_search` for a song by title + circle name extracted from YouTube title/description; returns structured match if found
- [ ] Playwright fallback: if OpenClaw unavailable, use `playwright.async_api` to navigate and scrape the same page deterministically
- [ ] Pydantic response model: `ArrangementChronicleResult` with same fields as TouhouDB result; shared `SongMetadata` base type

**LLM extractor**
- [ ] Implement `lotad/agents/llm_extractor.py` — `extract_metadata(item: PlaylistItem) -> SongMetadataExtraction` using direct Anthropic SDK (`anthropic.AsyncAnthropic`); structured output via `client.messages.create` with tool use / JSON mode
- [ ] `SongMetadataExtraction` Pydantic model: `title`, `arranger_names`, `vocalist_names`, `lyricist_names`, `circle_name`, `original_song_names`, `language`, `confidence: ConfidenceLevel`, `extraction_notes`
- [ ] Adversarial content guard: prompt instructs the model to flag if title/description looks intentionally misleading; low-confidence extractions surface `SUSPICIOUS_METADATA` task
- [ ] TouhouDB fuzzy validation: after LLM extraction, run `touhoudb_client.lookup_by_name(title, circle)` to cross-validate; mismatch → `SUSPICIOUS_METADATA` task; match → upsert with merged data

**Task system**
- [x] Create `lotad/tasks/manager.py` — `TaskManager.create(task_type, title, data, related_song_id=None, related_video_id=None)` writes to `tasks` table; idempotent (don't create duplicate OPEN task for same song+type)
- [x] Implement `lotad tasks list` — Rich table showing OPEN tasks sorted by priority; columns: id, type, title, created_at, related entity
- [x] Implement `lotad tasks show <id>` — full task detail with data payload formatted per task_type
- [x] Implement `lotad tasks resolve <id>` — guided interactive prompt per task_type; writes resolution data to DB; sets status = RESOLVED + resolved_at
- [x] Implement `lotad tasks dismiss <id>` — sets status = DISMISSED (used for false positives)
- [x] Task types implemented: `FILL_MISSING_INFO`, `DEDUPLICATE_SONGS`, `INGEST_FAILED`, `DROPPED_VIDEO` — with runbook-style resolution prompts. `MISSING_CIRCLE` was removed (most songs belong to a circle; noise outweighed value). `MISSING_LYRICIST` and `REVIEW_CHARACTER_MAPPING` resolution prompts still pending.

**Metadata integrity checks (added to ingest pipeline)**
- [x] Duration mismatch check: if YouTube `duration_seconds` vs TouhouDB `duration_seconds` differ by >20%, create `SUSPICIOUS_METADATA` task (do not block the write)
- [ ] Missing lyricist check: if `has_lyrics = true` and no `LYRICIST` rows in `song_artists`, create `MISSING_LYRICIST` task *(pending)*
- [x] ~~Missing circle check~~ — removed; see task types note above

---

#### Milestone 5 — Characters, Original Songs, and Normalization *(In Progress)*

**Character mapper**
- [x] `lotad originals scrape [--dry-run] [--limit N]` — fetch all Original-type songs by ZUN (artist_id=1) and U2 Akiyama (artist_id=45) from TouhouDB; upsert into `original_songs` with `touhoudb_id`, BPM, notes, stage (from tags); link characters at `confidence=MEDIUM` via `original_song_characters`; resolve open `FILL_MISSING_INFO` tasks; per-song commits with Rich progress bar
- [x] Schema migrations: 0006 (drop `track_number`/`is_extra_stage` from `original_songs`, move BPM fields there from `songs`), 0007 (add `works.touhoudb_id` for fast-path work matching), 0008 (`original_songs.work_id` nullable — songs from non-Touhou/Seihou works or unused tracks still land in the table)
- [x] Work matching for original song scrape: fast-path via `works.touhoudb_id`; year+`media_type` filter (prevents cross-type collisions); difflib name-similarity tiebreak (threshold ≥ 0.6); writes back `touhoudb_id` on first heuristic match
- [x] Seed Seihou games (Shuusou Gyoku `touhoudb_id=28`, Kioh Gyoku `touhoudb_id=136`) and missing books (Bohemian Archive in Japanese Red 2005; Strange and Bright Nature Deity vols 1–3 2008–2009)

**Data quality backfill** *(planned — to be tackled alongside the character mapper below, since both require external sources beyond TouhouDB)*

- [ ] **`original_songs.stage` backfill** — TouhouDB stage tags are only present on ~20–30% of songs; the remainder land with `stage = NULL`. Backfill by scraping Touhou Wiki track listings (`https://en.touhouwiki.net/wiki/<Game>/Music`) which have authoritative stage→track mappings for every game.
- [ ] **`original_songs.is_boss` backfill** — TouhouDB does not differentiate boss themes from stage themes; `is_boss` is currently always `false`. Backfill from the same Touhou Wiki track listings (boss themes are explicitly labelled).
- [ ] **`original_song_characters` confidence upgrade** — characters are currently seeded at `confidence=MEDIUM` from TouhouDB's character artist associations, which are sparse and sometimes wrong. Once `is_boss` and `stage` are backfilled, run the full `CharacterMapper` rules (boss rule → `HIGH`, stage rule → `MEDIUM` with task, LLM fallback → `LOW` with task) to replace the TouhouDB-seeded rows with higher-quality assignments and generate `REVIEW_CHARACTER_MAPPING` tasks for ambiguous cases.

- [ ] Implement `lotad/agents/character_mapper.py` — `CharacterMapper.populate_all()` iterates all `original_songs`, maps each to characters via rules + LLM fallback
- [ ] Boss theme rule: `original_songs.is_boss = true` → look up the character whose `character_works` entry has `appearance_type = BOSS` and `work_id` matches — auto-assign with `confidence = HIGH`
- [ ] Stage theme rule: stage theme (non-boss, not extra stage) → look up characters on that stage across all appearances — assign all with `confidence = MEDIUM`; surface `REVIEW_CHARACTER_MAPPING` task *(note: `is_extra_stage` column was dropped in migration 0006; extra stage is now encoded as `stage = 7`)*
- [ ] LLM fallback: for ambiguous or unnamed songs (e.g. staff roll themes, menu music), call Claude API with the song name + game name and a list of characters from that game; assign with `confidence = LOW`; always surface task
- [ ] Extra-stage and final boss handling: assign both stage theme and boss theme characters when applicable (e.g. Last Remote → Sanae + Koishi)
- [ ] Hifuu CD tracks: default-assign Maribel + Renko unless name strongly implies a specific character
- [ ] Original composition songs (`is_original_composition = true`): assign characters via `song_characters` join instead of `original_song_characters` (requirement 6.8)

**Normalization metrics**
- [ ] Implement `lotad/ingestion/normalization.py` — `NormalizationFetcher.refresh(entity_type, entity_id)` checks `normalization_metrics` table; if `last_fetched_at` > 24h ago, re-fetch from TouhouDB; upsert
- [ ] `get_or_refresh_all()` — iterate all original_songs + artists + circles in DB; lazy refresh those not fetched in 24h; respect circuit breaker
